text:
The seat is dying as a pricing unit, and most GTM teams haven't priced that reality in yet.

A seat is a proxy for value, built on the assumption that humans logging in equals work getting done. That assumption held for 20 years. It doesn't hold when one AI agent does the work of five SDRs and nobody logs in at all.

Most people read this as a packaging problem. Add a usage tier, bolt on an outcome SKU, keep the seat as the anchor. That misreads where buyers are headed. They're getting sharper about what they pay for, and "access for a human" is becoming the weakest value story you can tell.

My 12-24 month prediction: the fastest-growing B2B software companies in 2026 will look almost nothing like the PLG vs. sales-led debate we were having in 2023. They'll run three or four concurrent motions, price on consumption or outcomes, and treat seats as a legacy line item kept around for procurement. The pure self-serve playbook and the pure enterprise playbook will both feel dated.

If you're a PM or GTM leader, the work now isn't redesigning your pricing page. It's figuring out what unit of value your product would charge for if seats didn't exist. Runs? Decisions? Resolved tickets? Revenue influenced? Answer that, then back into packaging.

What's the unit of value your product would charge for if you couldn't count seats?

#SaaS #GTM #ProductStrategy #B2BSaaS #GrowthStrategy #ProductManagement
